does: VFUSE applies sparse autoencoders to diffusion-transformer activations in protein design models to identify and understand features associated with hazardous protein generation. The method trains SAEs on activations from RoseTTAFold3 and RFDiffusion3, then uses linear probes in the SAE latent space to detect hazardous designs with improved accuracy compared to probes fitted directly to original model representations. The approach discovers monosemantic features that selectively activate on hazardous protein designs with high specificity, achieving AUROC scores up to 0.84. This work demonstrates that SAE latent spaces can reveal interpretable hazard-related structure in protein diffusion models while maintaining model performance, establishing a mechanistic auditing framework for protein generation systems.
connects: This work builds on two major lines of research: mechanistic interpretability methods that use sparse autoencoders to decompose neural network representations into interpretable features, and safety auditing approaches for generative models. It extends SAE methodology, previously applied to transformer language models and vision systems, to the novel domain of all-atom diffusion transformers. The paper responds to growing concerns about dual-use risks in open-weight protein design models by combining interpretability techniques with domain-specific threat modeling, connecting interpretability goals to practical safety verification. It also builds on prior work in protein design safety and auditing generative models for harmful outputs, bringing mechanistic interpretability tools to bear on biological risk assessment in a way prior work has not systematically done.
enables: VFUSE establishes interpretable auditing as a feasible approach for protein design models, opening pathways toward safer deployment of generative biological systems. The discovery of monosemantic hazard-related features enables future work in feature-level safety interventions, such as targeted ablation or steering to reduce hazardous generation. This framework could extend to other diffusion-based generative models in biological and chemical domains, establishing interpretability as a standard auditing practice for high-stakes generative systems. The method also points toward using learned feature spaces as intermediate representations for other safety techniques like behavioral cloning or value learning. More broadly, VFUSE demonstrates how mechanistic interpretability can address real-world dual-use risks, potentially influencing how interpretability research engages with responsible AI deployment in biotechnology.
